summaryTemplate: {{className}} is the {{patternRole}}. The interface declares a virtual operation that concrete strategies override; clients hold a base reference and dispatch through it without knowing which concrete strategy is active. Pattern body lives at {{file}}:{{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
chosenRationaleTemplate: Tagged as Strategy because {{className}} sits inside an interface-and-implementations shape: a base class with a virtual method (lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}), and concrete subclasses that inherit and override that method. The variation point is *which algorithm runs*, decided by which concrete is plugged in at the call site.

strategy_interface_class.note: Strategy interface — defines the contract every concrete strategy must satisfy. Callers depend on this class, never on any concrete subclass directly.

strategy_virtual_marker.note: `virtual` keyword — without it the override below would shadow rather than dispatch, and the runtime swap that makes Strategy useful would not happen.

strategy_method.note: Strategy operation — this is the *one thing* concrete strategies vary. Adding more methods here grows the contract every concrete must implement, so the interface usually stays narrow on purpose.

strategy_concrete_class.note: Concrete Strategy — one of possibly several implementations of the interface. The caller's code does not change when a different concrete is plugged in; only this class's behavior takes over at runtime.

strategy_inheritance.note: Inheritance — the structural commitment to the interface contract. The override below has to match the virtual signature exactly; mismatches are caught by the compiler thanks to this relationship.

strategy_base_class.note: Base reference — names the interface this concrete satisfies. Removing it would make the override an unrelated method and break the runtime swap.

strategy_override_method.note: Override — the concrete behavior for this strategy. Two different concretes can disagree completely on what this method does as long as both honor the signature; that disagreement is the value of the pattern.

runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Decorator: Decorator also relies on an interface and inheritance, but it *wraps* an existing instance to add behavior. Strategy *replaces* behavior — there is no wrapped object at lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Adapter: Adapter conforms an existing class to a new interface for compatibility. Strategy presents multiple peer implementations of one interface chosen at runtime; the goal is variability, not compatibility.
